title: π and e — The Two Great Constants
unsafe_explanation: Scene functions use IIFE loops to compute partial sums for Leibniz series, Basel problem, and Taylor approximation of e^x.

scenes.title: Scene 1 — The Circle Constant π
scenes.description: π is the ratio of a circle's circumference to its diameter. We approach it three ways: geometrically via inscribed polygons, analytically via the Leibniz series, and via the Basel problem.
scenes.markdown:
# The Circle Constant π

π ≈ 3.14159265… is perhaps the most famous number in mathematics. It appears wherever circles appear — and in many places where circles seem to have nothing to do with the problem.

---

## Definition

For any circle with radius $r$:

$$C = 2\pi r \qquad A = \pi r^2$$

π is the *ratio* of circumference to diameter — a constant independent of the circle's size. That this ratio is the same for every circle is a non-obvious geometric fact.

---

## Archimedes' Method (c. 250 BC)

Archimedes computed π by squeezing a circle between inscribed and circumscribed regular polygons. For an **inscribed** regular $N$-gon of circumradius 1:

$$P_N = N \cdot 2\sin\!\frac{\pi}{N} \xrightarrow{N\to\infty} 2\pi$$

Each vertex lies on the unit circle. As $N$ grows, the polygon fills the circle and its perimeter approaches $2\pi$.

| $N$ | $P_N$ |
|---|---|
| 6 | 6.000000 |
| 12 | 6.211657 |
| 24 | 6.265257 |
| 96 | 6.282063 |
| ∞ | 6.283185… = 2π |

---

## Leibniz Series (1673)

Gottfried Leibniz discovered that π hides inside the simplest alternating sum:

$$\frac{\pi}{4} = 1 - \frac{1}{3} + \frac{1}{5} - \frac{1}{7} + \cdots = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{(-1)^k}{2k+1}$$

The convergence is painfully slow — you need thousands of terms for a few decimal places — but the formula is exact and beautiful.

---

## Basel Problem (Euler, 1734)

Leonhard Euler shocked the mathematical world by proving:

$$\sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{k^2} = 1 + \frac{1}{4} + \frac{1}{9} + \frac{1}{16} + \cdots = \frac{\pi^2}{6}$$

π appears in the sum of reciprocal squares — completely unexpected. The proof uses the fact that $\sin x / x$ can be written as an infinite product over its zeros $x = \pm k\pi$.

---

## Why π Appears Everywhere

π shows up far beyond geometry: in probability (the Gaussian), in number theory (the Basel problem), in Fourier analysis, and in quantum mechanics. Anywhere oscillation, rotation, or symmetry appears, π is nearby.

functions.name: leibnizExpanded
functions.expr: (()=>{const n=max(1,floor(N));let s='';const maxFull=6;const show=n<=maxFull?n:4;for(let k=0;k<show;k++){const d=2*k+1,pos=k%2===0;const frac=d===1?'1':'\\frac{1}{'+d+'}';s+=k===0?frac:(pos?'+':'-')+frac;}if(n>maxFull){const ns=show%2===0?'+':'-';const lk=n-1,ld=2*lk+1,lp=lk%2===0;s+=ns+'\\cdots'+(lp?'+':'-')+'\\frac{1}{'+ld+'}';}return s;})()

functions.name: baselExpanded
functions.expr: (()=>{const n=max(1,floor(N));let s='';const maxFull=5;const show=n<=maxFull?n:4;for(let k=1;k<=show;k++){const frac=k===1?'1':'\\frac{1}{'+k*k+'}';s+=k===1?frac:'+'+frac;}if(n>maxFull){s+='+\\cdots+\\frac{1}{'+n*n+'}';}return s;})()

steps.title: The Unit Circle
steps.description: π is defined as the ratio of a circle's circumference to its diameter — a constant that is the same for every circle regardless of size. For a unit circle ($r = 1$) the circumference is $C = 2\pi \approx 6.2832$ and the area is $A = \pi \approx 3.1416$. These two formulas are the reason π is fundamental to all of geometry.

steps.title: Archimedes' Method — N-gon Approximation
steps.description: Long before calculus, Archimedes trapped π between inscribed and circumscribed regular polygons. Each vertex of the inscribed $N$-gon sits exactly on the unit circle. The perimeter $P_N = N \cdot 2\sin(\pi/N)$ approaches $2\pi$ from below as $N$ grows. At $N = 6$ the hexagon gives $P = 6.000$; at $N = 96$ (Archimedes' own calculation) we already have $3.14103 < \pi < 3.14271$. Drag the slider and watch the polygon fill the circle.

steps.title: Leibniz Series — π from an Alternating Sum
steps.description: Leibniz discovered in 1673 that $\pi/4 = 1 - 1/3 + 1/5 - 1/7 + \cdots$ — π emerges from the reciprocals of odd integers with alternating signs. The blue curve shows all partial sums from 1 to 300; the partial sums oscillate above and below $\pi/4$ (the gold line), converging slowly. Drag $N$ and the orange dot tracks the current sum — you need roughly 300 terms for just 3 decimal places. Despite the slow convergence, the formula is exact: $\pi = 4\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}(-1)^k/(2k+1)$.

steps.title: Basel Problem — ∑ 1/n² = π²/6
steps.description: In 1734 Euler proved: $\sum_{k=1}^{\infty} 1/k^2 = \pi^2/6 \approx 1.6449$. The purple curve shows all partial sums from 1 to 500 — they grow quickly at first then level off near the gold target line $\pi^2/6$. Drag $N$ and the orange dot follows the current sum. Even at $N=500$ you're still off by about $0.002$. Euler's proof expanded $\sin(x)/x$ as an infinite product over its zeros and matched the $x^2$ coefficient — one of the most elegant arguments in all of mathematics.

scenes.title: Scene 2 — The Growth Constant e
scenes.description: e is the base of natural exponential growth. It appears as the limit of compound interest, as the unique base whose logarithm equals area under 1/x, and as the function equal to its own derivative.
scenes.markdown:
# The Growth Constant e

$e \approx 2.71828182845\ldots$ is the base of natural exponential growth and the foundation of calculus.

---

## Three Definitions — All Equivalent

**1. Compound interest limit**

$$e = \lim_{n\to\infty}\left(1+\frac{1}{n}\right)^n$$

Invest 1 unit at 100% annual interest compounded $n$ times per year; the balance after one year is $(1+1/n)^n$. As compounding becomes continuous ($n\to\infty$), the balance converges to $e$.

**2. Area under $1/x$**

$$\int_1^e \frac{1}{x}\,dx = 1$$

$e$ is the unique number such that the area under the hyperbola $y = 1/x$ from $x=1$ to $x=e$ equals exactly 1. This is also the definition of $\ln(e) = 1$.

**3. Self-derivative**

$$\frac{d}{dx}\,e^x = e^x$$

$e^x$ is the unique exponential function (up to scaling) that equals its own derivative. This is why $e$ is the natural base for calculus.

---

## Taylor Series

$$e^x = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\frac{x^k}{k!} = 1 + x + \frac{x^2}{2!} + \frac{x^3}{3!} + \cdots$$

The series converges for all $x$. The more terms you include, the wider the interval over which the approximation is accurate. At $x=0$, even 1 term is exact.

---

## Key Values

| Expression | Value |
|---|---|
| $e$ | $2.718281828\ldots$ |
| $e^2$ | $7.389056099\ldots$ |
| $1/e$ | $0.367879441\ldots$ |
| $\ln 2$ | $0.693147180\ldots$ |
| $e^{i\pi}$ | $-1$ (Euler's identity) |

functions.name: taylorExpanded
functions.expr: (()=>{const n=max(1,floor(N));const maxFull=5;const show=n<=maxFull?n:4;let s='';let fact=1;for(let k=0;k<show;k++){if(k>0)fact*=k;let term;if(k===0)term='1';else if(k===1)term='x';else term='\\frac{x^{'+k+'}}{'+fact+'}';s+=k===0?term:'+'+term;}if(n>maxFull){let lf=1;const lk=n-1;for(let j=1;j<=lk;j++)lf*=j;const lt=lk===1?'x':lk===0?'1':'\\frac{x^{'+lk+'}}{'+lf+'}';s+='+\\cdots+'+lt;}return s;})()

functions.name: taylorDerivExpanded
functions.expr: (()=>{const n=max(0,floor(N)-1);if(n<=0)return'0';const maxFull=5;const show=n<=maxFull?n:4;let s='';let fact=1;for(let k=0;k<show;k++){if(k>0)fact*=k;let term;if(k===0)term='1';else if(k===1)term='x';else term='\\frac{x^{'+k+'}}{'+fact+'}';s+=k===0?term:'+'+term;}if(n>maxFull){let lf=1;const lk=n-1;for(let j=1;j<=lk;j++)lf*=j;const lt=lk===1?'x':lk===0?'1':'\\frac{x^{'+lk+'}}{'+lf+'}';s+='+\\cdots+'+lt;}return s;})()

steps.title: Compound Interest Limit
steps.description: Invest 1 unit at 100% annual interest compounded $n$ times per year. After one year the balance is $(1+1/n)^n$. At $n=1$ (annual): 2.00. At $n=12$ (monthly): 2.613. At $n=365$ (daily): 2.7146. The limit as $n\to\infty$ is $e \approx 2.7183$ — continuous compounding. The blue curve $(1+1/n)^{nx}$ approaches the gold $e^x$ as you drag $n$ higher. The orange dot at $x=1$ shows the current value.

steps.title: Area Under 1/x
steps.description: The natural logarithm is defined as $\ln(b) = \int_1^b 1/x\,dx$ — the area under the hyperbola $y=1/x$ from 1 to $b$. This area equals exactly 1 when $b = e$. Drag the slider: below $e$ the area is less than 1, above $e$ it exceeds 1. The orange marker at $x = e \approx 2.718$ shows where the transition happens. This is not a coincidence — it's actually the geometric definition of $e$.

steps.title: $e^x$ Is Its Own Derivative — Taylor Series
steps.description: $e^x$ is the unique function satisfying $f'(x) = f(x)$. The Taylor series makes this visible: differentiate term by term — $\frac{d}{dx}\frac{x^k}{k!} = \frac{k\,x^{k-1}}{k!} = \frac{x^{k-1}}{(k-1)!}$ — and each term shifts one place to the left, reproducing the same series. The derivative of $N$ terms gives $N-1$ terms of the identical series; as $N\to\infty$ nothing is lost and $\frac{d}{dx}e^x = e^x$ exactly. Drag $N$ and watch the bottom overlay: the right-hand side is always the same sequence, just one term shorter.

scenes.title: Scene 3 — Where They Meet
scenes.description: π and e are not independent. They meet in Euler's formula e^(iθ) = cos θ + i sin θ — which unifies rotation, exponential growth, and the circle constant in one equation.
scenes.markdown:
# Where π and e Meet

π and e look like they come from completely different worlds — one from circles, one from growth. Yet they are deeply intertwined.

---

## Euler's Formula

$$e^{i\theta} = \cos\theta + i\sin\theta$$

This single equation connects:
- **e** — the base of natural growth
- **i** — the imaginary unit, $\sqrt{-1}$
- **π** — the circle constant
- **cos** and **sin** — the language of rotation

Plugging a purely imaginary number $i\theta$ into the exponential function produces a rotation by angle $\theta$ in the complex plane. The point $e^{i\theta}$ traces the unit circle.

---

## Euler's Identity

At $\theta = \pi$:

$$e^{i\pi} + 1 = 0$$

Five fundamental constants — $e$, $i$, $\pi$, $1$, $0$ — in one equation. It is often called the most beautiful equation in mathematics.

---

## The Gaussian Integral

$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-x^2}\,dx = \sqrt{\pi}$$

The integral of $e^{-x^2}$ (a bell curve) gives $\sqrt{\pi}$. The standard proof squares the integral:

$$\left(\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-x^2}\,dx\right)^2 = \iint e^{-(x^2+y^2)}\,dA$$

Switching to polar coordinates $r, \theta$:

$$= \int_0^{2\pi}\int_0^{\infty} e^{-r^2}\,r\,dr\,d\theta = 2\pi \cdot \frac{1}{2} = \pi$$

The $2\pi$ from the angular integral is exactly the circumference of the unit circle — π sneaks in through the back door.

---

## The Normal Distribution

$$f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}\,e^{-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}}$$

Normalizing the Gaussian gives the bell curve. Both $e$ (in the exponent) and $\pi$ (in the normalization) appear together. The $\sqrt{2\pi}$ factor ensures $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x)\,dx = 1$.

### Empirical Rule

| Band | Probability |
|---|---|
| $\mu \pm \sigma$ | 68.3% |
| $\mu \pm 2\sigma$ | 95.4% |
| $\mu \pm 3\sigma$ | 99.7% |

steps.title: e^(iθ) Traces the Unit Circle
steps.description: Plug a purely imaginary number $i\theta$ into $e$ and the result is a rotation: $e^{i\theta} = \cos\theta + i\sin\theta$. The gold vector has real part $\cos\theta$ (blue projection onto Re axis) and imaginary part $\sin\theta$ (pink projection onto Im axis). As $\theta$ increases from 0 to $2\pi$, the tip traces the entire unit circle. Press **Animate** to watch it rotate continuously.

steps.title: Euler's Identity — e^(iπ) + 1 = 0
steps.description: Set $\theta = \pi$: the rotating vector lands exactly on $-1$. The green arrow adds $+1$, bringing it back to the origin. Five of the most fundamental constants — $e$ (growth), $i$ (rotation), $\pi$ (circle), $1$ (identity), $0$ (origin) — united in one equation: $e^{i\pi} + 1 = 0$. Richard Feynman called it "the most remarkable formula in mathematics."

steps.title: The Gaussian Integral — ∫e^(−x²)dx = √π
steps.description: The bell-shaped surface $z = e^{-(x^2+y^2)}$ has total volume exactly $\pi$. The gold cross-section shows $e^{-x^2}$ along the x-axis — integrating it gives $\sqrt{\pi}$. The proof squares the integral, rewrites the double integral in polar coordinates, and the $2\pi$ from the full rotation around the z-axis delivers $\pi$. π enters through the circular symmetry of the Gaussian — the same circle constant that appeared in Archimedes' polygons.

steps.title: The Normal Distribution — π and e Together
steps.description: Normalize $e^{-x^2}$ by $1/(\sigma\sqrt{2\pi})$ to get a probability distribution that integrates to 1. Both $e$ and $\pi$ appear in the formula — $e$ shapes the curve, $\pi$ calibrates the area. The three blue bands show the empirical rule: $\mu \pm \sigma$ contains 68.3% of probability, $\mu \pm 2\sigma$ contains 95.4%, $\mu \pm 3\sigma$ contains 99.7%. Drag $\mu$ to shift the distribution and $\sigma$ to widen or narrow it.
